mission.title: Install and Configure Kairos on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kairos is an immutable Linux meta-distribution designed for edge Kubernetes, enabling the creation of bootable Kubernetes and OS images for edge devices. Installing Kairos allows you to manage cluster lifecycle seamlessly and securely, ensuring no infrastructure drift.

steps.title: Add the Kairos Helm repository
steps.description:
To install Kairos, first add the official Helm repository:
```bash
helm repo add kairos https://charts.kairos.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kairos using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Kairos with the following command, creating a namespace called 'kairos':
```bash
helm install kairos kairos/kairos --namespace kairos --create-namespace --version v4.0.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kairos pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kairos
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Kairos deployment. Edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kairos --namespace kairos
``` 
Add the following under 'spec.template.spec.containers.resources':
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Kairos service, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kairos --namespace kairos 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kairos will show the Kairos pods running in the 'kairos' namespace. You should also be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kairos https://charts.kairos.io
helm repo update
helm install kairos kairos/kairos --namespace kairos --create-namespace --version v4.0.0

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Kairos installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kairos --namespace kairos
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kairos | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace and verify that all resources have been cleaned up:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kairos
kubectl get all --namespace kairos
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of the Kairos installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kairos -o yaml > kairos-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kairos installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kairos kairos/kairos --namespace kairos --version v4.1.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Kairos pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kairos
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see that the Kairos pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kairos
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kairos
``` 
If the service is not running, you may need to restart the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource usage and limits:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kairos --namespace kairos
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that you are in the correct namespace and that the release name is correct:
```bash
helm list --namespace kairos
``` 
If the release is not listed, it may have been uninstalled or not installed correctly.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
